title: Minimal spin-rotor model for Barnett and Einstein--de Haas physics
abstract: The Barnett effect is usually understood through an effective magnetic field generated by mechanical rotation, while its reciprocal Einstein--de Haas effect describes the transfer of spin angular momentum to mechanical motion. We show that this effective-field picture changes qualitatively once the mechanical degree of freedom itself is quantized. To demonstrate this, we introduce an exactly solvable minimal spin-rotor model in which a spin-$1/2$ is coupled to a quantum rotor. In a fixed angular-momentum sector, the model reproduces the conventional Barnett splitting and remains formally equivalent to a Zeeman problem. For a superposition of rotor sectors, however, the Barnett field becomes operator-valued and the resulting dynamics generates coherent spin-rotor entanglement. This is directly visible in the reduced spin purity, rotor coherence, and entanglement entropy. Our results identify a minimal quantum setting in which the Barnett effective-field picture departs from its classical form and acquires a reciprocal manifestation through spin-dependent rotor coherence.
summary: This paper explores the quantum limits of the Barnett and Einstein-de Haas effects. By using a minimal model of a spin-1/2 particle coupled to a quantum rotor, the author demonstrates that quantizing the rotation transforms the Barnett field from a static magnetic field into an operator-valued field. This transition leads to the creation of entanglement between the spin and the rotor. The results show that the classical effective-field picture fails qualitatively in the quantum regime, replaced by a reciprocal relationship between spin dynamics and rotor coherence.
main_problem: Investigating whether the classical effective-field description of the Barnett effect remains valid when the mechanical rotation is quantized.
main_result: The study shows that quantizing the rotor causes the Barnett field to become operator-valued, leading to the generation of coherent spin-rotor entanglement and a breakdown of the classical Zeeman-like analogy.
method: The author uses an exact analytical solution of a spin-rotor Hamiltonian, calculating eigenenergies, time-evolution, and entanglement metrics like spin purity and entanglement entropy.
model_or_system: A minimal, exactly solvable model consisting of a spin-1/2 particle coupled to a planar quantum rotor with a Hamiltonian containing spin-rotation coupling.
key_observables: Reduced spin purity (P_spin), entanglement entropy (S_ent), rotor coherence (|K(t)|), and spin-state-dependent phase evolution.
important_parameters: The coupling parameter eta = gm/Delta, which defines weak-coupling (eta < 1), balanced (eta = 1), and large-coupling (eta > 1) regimes.
main_assumptions: The model is a minimal setting; the author notes that adding more degrees of freedom (like angular dependence) is a potential extension.
figures_summary: Fig 1(a) shows the energy spectrum and Barnett-split branches; Fig 1(b) shows the time dependence of spin purity for different coupling strengths; Fig 1(c) shows the periodic buildup and decay of entanglement entropy.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical problem of the Barnett effect, presents the minimal spin-rotor model, provides an exact analytical solution, analyzes the quantum-mechanical breakdown of the effective-field picture through entanglement and purity, and concludes with the identification of a reciprocal quantum manifestation of the effect.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental look at how the quantization of a mechanical degree of freedom fundamentally changes the nature of an effective field, moving from a static parameter to an operator that generates entanglement.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Exact spectrum ε± m of the minimal spin-rotor model as a function of the rotor angular-momentum quantum number m, showing the Barnett-split branches around the underlying rotor dispersion. (b) Time dependence of the reduced spin purity Pspin(t) for m = 2, 4, 8, corresponding to η = gm/∆= 0.5, 1, 2 for the parameters used here. These three cases illustrate the weak-coupling, maximally entangling, and large-η regimes, respectively. The purity reduction is maximal near the balanced point η = 1, while the cases η and 1/η have the same minimum purity but different oscillation frequencies. The reduction of Pspin(t) below unity signals the onset of coherent spin-rotor entanglement. (c)...
